matter.title: Roe v. Maria Q. Fairbanks et al. (Intertidal / Shoreland Access)
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe, owner of a rocky ledge shoreline and mudflats, sues Maria Q. Fairbanks and abutter Ingrid B. Kingsley to confirm ownership of the intertidal flats to the low-water mark and to stop beachcombing and picnicking by the public, which the defendants defend as a prescriptive right from decades of use.

defendant.role: Defendant (a user of the flats: harvester/tour operator)

defendant_2.role: Defendant (an abutting owner claiming a right of access)

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe, owner of a rocky ledge shoreline and mudflats, sues Maria Q. Fairbanks and abutter Ingrid B. Kingsley to confirm ownership of the intertidal flats to the low-water mark and to stop beachcombing and picnicking by the public, which the defendants defend as a prescriptive right from decades of use.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe owns shorefront property consisting of a rocky ledge shoreline and mudflats on the coast of Sagadahoc County, Maine. Under Maine's colonial ordinance and the deed in the chain of title, the plaintiff claims ownership of the intertidal flats seaward to the low-water mark -- here about 90 rods of flats -- subject only to the public's reserved easement for fishing, fowling, and navigation recognized in the Moody Beach case.

The dispute centers on beachcombing and picnicking by the public. Maria Q. Fairbanks carries on that activity across the plaintiff's flats, and the abutting owner, Ingrid B. Kingsley, claims a separate right of access over the flats. The defendants assert that the use is permitted as a prescriptive right from decades of use. Grace contends the activity exceeds the narrow public easement -- which is limited to fishing, fowling, and navigation -- and is an unlawful intrusion onto privately owned intertidal land.

Matters came to a head around 2025-05-15. The Town of Semaphore Cove has an interest in the public's rights and the status of an abutting land trust parcel. Grace now seeks a declaration of ownership of the flats to the low-water mark, an order quieting title against the abutter's claimed access, and an injunction barring the disputed use, while respecting any genuine fishing, fowling, and navigation rights of the public.
undisputed_facts: Grace C. Roe owns shorefront property consisting of a rocky ledge shoreline and mudflats on the coast of Sagadahoc County.
The defendants engage in beachcombing and picnicking by the public on or across the intertidal flats fronting the plaintiff's land.
The colonial ordinance reserves to the public an easement for fishing, fowling, and navigation over privately owned intertidal flats.
disputed_facts: Whether the plaintiff's title under the colonial ordinance reaches the flats to the low-water mark, up to the 100-rod limit.
Whether beachcombing and picnicking by the public falls within the public's reserved fishing, fowling, and navigation easement or exceeds it as a trespass to the privately owned flats.
Whether the defendants hold a prescriptive easement or customary public right to use or cross the flats.

timeline.event: The disputed use of the flats first led to confrontation between the parties.
Complaint to quiet title and for declaratory and injunctive relief prepared for filing in Bath Superior Court.

third_parties.name: Rosa T. Birchwood
third_parties.relevance: Speaks to navigation, mooring, and access along the shore.

third_parties.name: Maine Department of Marine Resources
third_parties.relevance: Licenses harvesting and regulates activity in the intertidal zone.

third_parties.name: Nadia S. Loe
third_parties.relevance: Speaks to the nature and ecological effect of harvesting or use on the intertidal flats.

exchanges.question: What are you hoping to accomplish?
exchanges.answer: I want it confirmed that I own the flats out to the low-water mark, and I want beachcombing and picnicking by the public to stop. I am not trying to keep anyone from fishing, fowling, or navigating -- just from treating my flats as their own.

exchanges.question: Tell me about your shorefront.
exchanges.answer: It is a rocky ledge shoreline and mudflats. My deed and the old colonial ordinance run my ownership down to the low-water mark -- the survey says about 90 rods of flats. I have owned and paid taxes on it for years.

exchanges.question: What exactly are the defendants doing?
exchanges.answer: Mostly beachcombing and picnicking by the public. One of them is an abutter, Ingrid, who says he has a right to cross my flats. They claim it is all allowed as a prescriptive right from decades of use.

exchanges.question: How long has this been going on, and was there any confrontation?
exchanges.answer: It came to a head around 2025-05-15. I have tried to handle it neighborly, but it keeps happening across my flats, almost every low tide in season.

exchanges.question: Where does the Town fit in?
exchanges.answer: The Town of Semaphore Cove cares about public access -- there is an abutting land trust parcel. I respect real fishing, fowling, and navigation rights; I just do not think this use qualifies.

exchanges.question: How do you want to handle it?
exchanges.answer: I would rather settle than wage war on the neighbors, but I need clear lines: my title to the low-water mark confirmed, the unwanted use stopped, and the public easement honored where it really applies.

goals: Confirm the plaintiff's ownership of the intertidal flats to the low-water mark under the colonial ordinance.
Stop beachcombing and picnicking by the public to the extent it exceeds the public's fishing, fowling, and navigation easement.
Quiet title against the abutter Ingrid B. Kingsley's claimed right of access over the flats.
priorities: A clear adjudication that the flats are privately owned to the low-water mark.
An injunction that stops the unwanted use without trampling genuine public rights.
Preserving good relations with the Town and lawful fishing, fowling, and navigation.
desired_outcomes: A declaratory and quiet-title judgment confirming ownership of the flats to the low-water mark and rejecting the abutter's claimed access.
An injunction barring the disputed use to the extent it exceeds the public easement, while respecting fishing, fowling, and navigation.
non_starters: Any ruling that treats the privately owned flats as open to general public recreation beyond fishing, fowling, and navigation.

constraints.budget: Moderate; cost-conscious but committed to confirming title to the flats.
constraints.timeline: Wants ownership of the flats and the scope of the public easement settled before the next harvesting and tourist season.

issues.issue: Whether the defendants hold a prescriptive easement or customary public right over the flats
sub_questions: Was the use open, notorious, and continuous for the prescriptive period?
Does any customary public right reach beyond fishing, fowling, and navigation?
issues.client_position: No prescriptive easement or customary right has ripened over the plaintiff's flats.

issues.issue: Trespass to the privately owned intertidal flats
sub_questions: Does the activity intrude on land the plaintiff owns to the low-water mark?
Is the intrusion privileged by the public easement?
issues.client_position: Use exceeding fishing, fowling, and navigation is a trespass to the plaintiff's flats.

issues.issue: Declaratory judgment and quiet title as to ownership of the flats and the scope of public rights
sub_questions: Is a declaration appropriate to fix the boundary at the low-water mark and the easement's scope?
Should title be quieted against the abutter's claimed access?

issues.issue: Whether the disputed use falls within or outside the public's reserved fishing, fowling, and navigation easement
issues.governing_law: Bell v. Town of Wells; Ross v. Acadian Seaplants
sub_questions: Is the disputed use one of fishing, fowling, or navigation?
Does commercial rockweed harvesting or general recreation exceed the reserved easement?
issues.client_position: The disputed use exceeds the narrow public easement and intrudes on the privately owned flats.

expert_opinions.topic: The nature and effect of the use on the intertidal flats
expert_opinions.opinion: The activity is a customary marine use consistent with fishing, fowling, and navigation in the intertidal zone.
expert_opinions.basis: Field observation of the flats and study of the harvesting or access activity.

evidence.title: Cease-and-desist correspondence
evidence.description: Cease-and-desist letters and correspondence demanding that the disputed use stop.

evidence.title: DMR licensing records for harvesting
evidence.description: Department of Marine Resources licensing records for any harvesting on the flats.

evidence.title: Photographs of the disputed activity on the flats
evidence.description: Photographs of the disputed use taking place on the intertidal flats.

evidence.title: Photographs of the posted no-trespassing signage
evidence.description: Photographs of the no-trespassing signs posted at the shorefront.

amounts.label: Claimed harvesting revenue at stake
amounts.basis: Estimated annual revenue from the disputed harvesting on the flats.

amounts.label: Cost of survey and title work
amounts.basis: Cost of the tidal boundary survey and the colonial-ordinance title abstract.

causes_of_action.claim: Declaratory judgment that the plaintiff owns the flats to the low-water mark and the disputed use exceeds the public easement
causes_of_action.against: Maria Q. Fairbanks, Ingrid B. Kingsley, and the Town of Semaphore Cove
causes_of_action.governing_law: Bell v. Town of Wells; the colonial ordinance; 14 M.R.S. § 5951 et seq.
elements: Ownership of the upland and the flats to the low-water mark under the colonial ordinance
A public easement limited to fishing, fowling, and navigation
The disputed use exceeds that reserved easement
An actual controversy ripe for declaration
causes_of_action.relief_sought: A declaration that the plaintiff owns the flats to the low-water mark and that the disputed use exceeds the public easement

elements: The plaintiff owns the flats to the low-water mark
The defendant intruded on the privately owned flats
The intrusion is not privileged by fishing, fowling, or navigation
Resulting injury to the plaintiff's possession
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Damages and an order ending the trespass to the intertidal flats

causes_of_action.claim: Quiet title against the abutter's claimed access
elements: The plaintiff owns the flats to the low-water mark
The abutter asserts an adverse claim of access over the flats
The adverse claim is without basis in the record title
Title should be quieted against the claim
causes_of_action.relief_sought: A judgment quieting title against the abutter's claimed right of access over the flats

elements: The plaintiff owns the flats and the use exceeds the public easement
The continuing use causes irreparable harm
The balance of harms favors the plaintiff
There is no adequate remedy at law
causes_of_action.relief_sought: An order enjoining the disputed use of the flats to the extent it exceeds the public easement

affirmative_defenses.defense: The plaintiff's title does not reach the flats
affirmative_defenses.basis: The chain of title does not convey the intertidal flats to the low-water mark to the plaintiff under the colonial ordinance.
Decades of open and continuous public use of the flats establish a prescriptive easement or a customary public right. 14 M.R.S. § 812.

counterclaims.claim: Declaration of a public or prescriptive right to use the flats
counterclaims.relief: A declaration that the defendants and the public hold a fishing/fowling/navigation, prescriptive, or customary right to use the intertidal flats.

motions.title: Motion for a preliminary injunction
motions.relief: A preliminary injunction limiting the disputed use of the flats pending judgment.

motions.title: Motion for summary judgment on title
motions.relief: Summary judgment that the plaintiff owns the flats to the low-water mark under the colonial ordinance.

docket.entry: Complaint to quiet title and for declaratory and injunctive relief filed.
Answer and counterclaim asserting public and prescriptive rights filed.
Answer of the abutting owner on the claimed access filed.
Order on the motion for a preliminary injunction entered.
Scheduling order entered and the Town appears on the public's rights.
Hearing held on summary judgment as to title to the flats.

facts.shorefront_type: a rocky ledge shoreline and mudflats
facts.disputed_use: beachcombing and picnicking by the public
facts.public_use_claimed: a prescriptive right from decades of use
